title: want + someone + to + verb
rule: After 'want', a person is followed by 'to' + base verb.
right: I want you to build it.
examples: I want you to consider re-rendering for optimization.
She wants me to call her tomorrow.
They want us to finish by Friday.

title: talk with / to someone
rule: Use the preposition 'with' or 'to' before a person.
right: I want to talk with people.
examples: Can I talk to you for a second?
I talked with my teacher about it.

title: every + singular noun
rule: 'every' is always followed by a singular noun.
examples: I study every day.
Every person is different.

title: make a mistake (not 'do')
rule: We 'make' mistakes, decisions, and plans; we 'do' homework and work.
examples: Don't worry if you make a mistake.
I need to make a decision soon.

title: Articles with singular countable nouns
rule: A singular countable noun needs an article: a / an / the.
wrong: You can use state management library.
right: You can use a state management library.
examples: I bought a book.
She is an engineer.

title: whether (~인지 아닌지)
rule: Use 'whether' (or 'if') to turn a yes/no question into part of a sentence.
wrong: I don't know it will sync.
right: I don't know whether it will sync.
examples: This decides whether your data follows you across devices.
It depends on whether you study every day.

title: preposition + -ing
rule: After a preposition (at, in, about, of, for, on…), use the -ing form, not the base verb.
examples: I'm interested in learning Japanese.
Thank you for helping me.
She's thinking about changing jobs.

title: Giving opinions: I think / In my opinion / I'd say
rule: Use 'I think...', 'I believe...', 'I feel...' + a sentence, or 'In my opinion,' / 'From my perspective,' at the start of a sentence. Don't say 'My opinion is that…' — it sounds stiff.
wrong: My opinion is the daily practice is the best way to improve.
right: In my opinion, daily practice is the best way to improve.
examples: I think consistency matters more than studying for long hours.
I'd say speaking with native speakers is the fastest way to learn.
From my perspective, making mistakes is how we grow.
I believe everyone can become fluent with the right habits.

title: Self-correction: Actually… / What I meant was…
rule: When you say something wrong or unclear, correct yourself naturally: 'Actually,', 'What I meant was...', 'Let me rephrase that...', or 'I mean...'. Don't stop — keeping the flow matters more than perfection.
wrong: I think English is... [long silence, give up]
right: I think English is — actually, what I meant is I find it challenging but enjoyable.
examples: Wait, let me rephrase that — I mean I enjoy grammar, not that I'm already good at it.
She is — I mean, she was — my teacher last year.
I've studied English for... actually, it's been about five years.

title: used to + verb (과거 습관)
rule: Use 'used to + base verb' to describe a past habit or state that no longer exists. Don't confuse with 'be used to + -ing' (= accustomed to, still true now).
wrong: I use to study English every day.
right: I used to study English every day, but now I study on my own.
examples: I used to be nervous about speaking, but now I'm more confident.
She used to live in Seoul before she moved abroad.
We used to have English lessons twice a week at school.

title: Making suggestions: should / Why don't you / How about / You might want to
rule: To give advice or make suggestions, use: (1) 'should + base verb' — general advice; (2) 'Why don't you/we + base verb?' — friendly suggestion; (3) 'How about + -ing?' — casual idea; (4) 'You might want to + base verb' — polite, softer advice. After 'Why don't you/we', do NOT add 'to'.
wrong: Why don't you to try speaking with a tutor?
right: Why don't you try speaking with a tutor?
examples: You should review vocabulary every day, not just before a test.
Why don't we watch an English drama together?
How about keeping a small notebook for new words?
You might want to slow down a little — clarity matters more than speed.

title: Present perfect: have/has + past participle
rule: Use 'have/has + past participle' for: (1) life experiences without a specific time ('I've visited Japan'), (2) recent events still connected to now ('I've just finished'), (3) situations that started in the past and continue now ('I've studied English for five years'). Do NOT use present perfect with finished-time words like 'yesterday', 'last year', 'in 2020' — switch to simple past then.
wrong: I study English for five years.
right: I've studied English for five years. (started in the past, still continuing → present perfect)
examples: I've visited Japan twice — it was amazing both times.
Have you ever tried speaking English with a complete stranger?
She hasn't missed a single practice session this month.
I've just realised I was pronouncing that word wrong the whole time.

title: 2nd conditional: If + past simple → would + base verb
rule: Use the 2nd conditional for unreal or imaginary present/future situations: 'If + past simple, would + base verb'. Do NOT put 'would' in the if-clause. The past tense signals the situation is hypothetical — not that it happened in the past.
wrong: If I would have more time, I will study English every day.
right: If I had more time, I would study English every day.
examples: If I lived abroad for a year, I would become much more fluent.
What would you do if you could speak English perfectly?
I would travel the world if I had enough money.
If I weren't so busy, I would dedicate more time to speaking practice.

title: Future plans: be going to / planning to / intending to
rule: Use 'be going to + base verb' for plans already decided before the moment of speaking. Use 'will + base verb' for spontaneous decisions made right now. Use 'planning to + verb', 'thinking of + -ing', or 'intending to + verb' as natural alternatives for pre-decided intentions. Never say 'I am go to' or 'I will going to'.
wrong: I will visit Japan next month. (you already booked the tickets)
right: I'm going to visit Japan next month. (plan was already decided)
examples: I'm going to start a speaking course next month — I've already signed up.
A: We're out of coffee. B: Don't worry, I'll grab some on the way home. (spontaneous → will)
I'm planning to join a language exchange group once I feel more confident.
I'm thinking of signing up for an online English class — have you tried one?

title: Concession: although / even though vs. despite / in spite of
rule: Use 'although' or 'even though' before a full clause (subject + verb). Use 'despite' or 'in spite of' before a noun or -ing form — never before a full clause. A common mistake is writing 'Despite I was...' — 'despite' needs a noun or -ing after it.
wrong: Despite I was nervous, I managed to speak in front of the class.
right: Although I was nervous, I managed to speak. / Despite being nervous, I managed to speak.
examples: Even though I make mistakes, I keep on practising every day.
Despite studying hard, I still struggle with pronunciation.
Although English grammar is complex, it gets easier with practice.
I enjoy speaking, even though I'm not yet fluent.

title: Relative clauses: who / which / that
rule: A relative clause describes a noun by adding information about it. Use 'who' for people, 'which' for things, and 'that' for either (common in conversation). Place the relative pronoun directly after the noun it describes. Do NOT add an extra pronoun inside the clause ('The person who he helped me' is wrong — 'he' is redundant). Do NOT use 'what' as a relative pronoun.
wrong: The book what I'm reading is really helpful.
right: The book that I'm reading is really helpful.
examples: The person who helped me the most was my English teacher.
I love films that make you think about life.
The method which works best for me is listening to podcasts.
Do you remember the word that came up in our last session?

title: Reported speech: say (that) / tell someone (that)
rule: When reporting what someone said, shift the tense back: present → past, will → would, can → could. Use 'tell + person + (that)' or 'say + (that)' — never 'say + person'. The word 'that' is optional in spoken English. Also update pronouns: 'I' → 'he/she', 'my' → 'his/her'.
wrong: She said me that she likes learning English.
right: She told me that she liked learning English. ('tell + person'; likes → liked)
examples: He said that he was practising speaking every morning.
She told me that she couldn't come to the study session.
They said the new method would be more effective.
My teacher told us that making mistakes is the best way to learn.

title: Passive voice: be + past participle
rule: Use the passive voice when the action matters more than who did it, or when the doer is unknown or obvious. Form: subject + am/is/are + past participle (present) or was/were + past participle (past). To mention the doer, add 'by + agent'. Common mistake: using the simple past form instead of the past participle after 'be' (e.g. 'was wrote' instead of 'was written').
wrong: This book was wrote by a famous author.
right: This book was written by a famous author. (write → written, not 'wrote')
examples: English is spoken in more than 50 countries.
The new app was released last week.
I was told that the meeting had been cancelled.
The report will be published on Friday.

title: Question tags: isn't it? / don't you? / haven't you?
rule: A question tag is a short yes/no question added to the end of a statement to check information or invite agreement. Rule: positive statement → negative tag; negative statement → positive tag. The tag must use the SAME auxiliary verb (is/are/do/does/did/have/has/can/will) and subject pronoun as the main sentence. The most common Korean-English mistake is using 'isn't it?' for every sentence — only use it when the main verb is a form of 'be'.
wrong: You study English every day, isn't it?
right: You study English every day, don't you? (match the auxiliary: 'study' → 'do', not 'is')
examples: She has studied abroad before, hasn't she?
You can speak Japanese, can't you?
They didn't cancel the meeting, did they?
It's not as hard as it looks, is it?
